title: About This Site
text:
This website is a working handbook for data science in the Golden Lab. It is designed for students and collaborators who need practical guidance on:
The site was expanded from the ideas in presentation.qmd, then reorganized into documentation pages so that the material is easier to revisit after onboarding.

title: About This Site
text:
What this site is for
Use this handbook when you need a lab default, a project scaffold, a pointer to the right infrastructure, or a concise explanation of why a workflow recommendation exists.

title: About This Site
text:
What this site is not
This is not a substitute for official FASRC policy, study-specific data use agreements, or project-specific SOPs. When those sources disagree with a local habit, the official requirement wins.

title: About This Site
text:
How to contribute
The best contributions are:

corrections to outdated instructions,
clearer examples,
additions to the data and workflow guides,
and links to lab-specific templates that save other people time.

When in doubt, prefer small improvements and clear examples over long policy text.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Version control is not only for backup. It is how the lab understands change, reviews methods, and avoids conflicting local copies of the same project.
The presentation frames Git and GitHub as the tool for traceability, and that is exactly right: commit history should explain what changed, when, and why.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Version control is not only for backup. It is how the lab understands change, reviews methods, and avoids conflicting local copies of the same project.
The presentation frames Git and GitHub as the tool for traceability, and that is exactly right: commit history should explain what changed, when, and why.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Daily Git habits
Use Git for active work, not just for milestone dumps.

Create the repository early.
Commit small, coherent changes.
Use commit messages that describe the decision or milestone.
Push regularly so the work is visible and recoverable.
Keep data out of Git unless policy clearly allows it.

The NSAPH handbook also recommends pushing code regularly, especially before asking for help or presenting work.1

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Branches and pull requests
For solo projects, a lightweight branch workflow is still useful:

Make a branch for risky or substantial work.
Open a pull request when the method or output changes meaningfully.
Use review comments to discuss code, assumptions, and documentation gaps.
Merge only when the branch leaves the repository in a reproducible state.

title: Collaboration and Support
section: What a good commit message looks like
text:
What a good commit message looks like
Better:

add first pass of temperature exposure merge
document MCBS variable cleaning assumptions
move repeated model code into helper function

Worse:

updates
stuff
final final version

title: Collaboration and Support
section: How to ask for help effectively
text:
How to ask for help effectively
R for Data Science recommends preparing a minimal reproducible example when you are stuck.2 That is one of the highest-leverage habits students can learn.
When asking the RSE or another lab member for help, include:

the repo or branch,
the exact script or notebook,
the command you ran,
the full error message,
the smallest reproducible example you can produce,
and any data access constraints that prevent direct debugging.

title: Collaboration and Support
section: What to send before a meeting
text:
What to send before a meeting
For technical meetings, send:

the current notebook or report,
a short status note,
open questions,
and links to the relevant code or pull request.

This shifts meetings away from “what happened?” and toward “what decision do we need to make?”

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Code review checklist
Review for:

reproducibility,
path portability,
data leakage risk,
hidden assumptions,
duplicated code,
and missing documentation.

Do not review only for style.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Support ladder
Use this order when possible:

Check the project README and this site.
Search the official docs for the tool you are using.
Reduce the problem to a minimal example.
Open an issue or send a focused help request.
Escalate to synchronous help when the blocker persists.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
The real goal
The goal is not to make every student a Git expert. The goal is to make the lab’s work inspectable, shareable, and recoverable.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
R for Data Science recommends one project per analysis and treating the project directory as the analysis home.1 That is the cleanest default for lab work too.
A project should contain everything required to understand and rerun the code except restricted data that must stay in approved shared locations.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
R for Data Science recommends one project per analysis and treating the project directory as the analysis home.1 That is the cleanest default for lab work too.
A project should contain everything required to understand and rerun the code except restricted data that must stay in approved shared locations.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
section: Scripts are the source of truth
text:
Scripts are the source of truth
The most important R4DS workflow recommendation is simple: for larger projects and collaboration, the source of truth should be the scripts, because scripts plus data recreate the environment.2
That generalizes beyond R:

in R, keep the workflow in scripts, Quarto notebooks, and package lockfiles,
in Python, keep it in scripts, notebooks, pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, or environment.yml,
and in either language, avoid workflows that depend on an IDE’s memory of prior objects.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Relative paths only
R4DS is explicit here: once you are inside a project, use relative paths, not absolute paths, because absolute paths hinder sharing.3
Good:
readr::read_csv(here::here("data", "analytic", "cohort.csv"))
Bad:
readr::read_csv("/Users/alice/Desktop/final_data/cohort.csv")

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
A practical project layout
project-name/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── data/
├── data-raw/
├── code/
├── notebooks/
├── reports/
├── figures/
├── results/
├── scratch/
└── renv.lock
Suggested meanings:

data/: pointers, symlinks, small metadata, and lightweight project-owned data
data-raw/: import receipts, extraction scripts, or manifests for original inputs
code/: reusable scripts and functions
notebooks/: exploratory and explanatory Quarto notebooks
reports/: rendered outputs for meetings, manuscripts, or handoff
results/: small derived artifacts worth keeping
scratch/: local pointers to transient compute outputs when appropriate

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Notebook versus script versus function
Use a notebook when you need to explain reasoning.
Use a script when you need to run a stable step repeatedly.
Use a function when logic is reused in more than one place.
Use a pipeline when many steps depend on each other and reruns are expensive.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Environment management
The presentation already points to renv and conda, and that is still the right baseline.
For R:
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
For Python, commit an environment.yml or pyproject.toml and keep it current.
The goal is not perfection. The goal is that another person, or future you, can reconstruct the environment without guesswork.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Configuration, not hard-coded infrastructure
If a project depends on shared paths outside the repository, put those paths in configuration.
default:
  analytic_root: "/n/shared/project-data"
  scratch_root: "/n/netscratch/project-name"
Then use that configuration in code instead of repeating infrastructure paths throughout the analysis.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
section: Save outputs from code, not with the mouse
text:
Save outputs from code, not with the mouse
If a figure matters, it should be saved from the script or notebook that created it. R4DS makes this point for figures, but the same logic applies to tables, model objects, and reports: reproducible export beats manual copy-paste every time.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
When to move beyond notebooks
Move to a pipeline or more modular structure when:

the same notebook is rerun many times with small edits,
multiple students touch the same workflow,
the compute cost makes reruns expensive,
or you can no longer tell which cells are still authoritative.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
A strong minimum standard
Every active project should have:

a readable README,
a committed .gitignore,
a current notebook or report,
relative paths,
and an environment specification.

The point of the workflow is not bureaucracy. The point is to make a project understandable and rerunnable at every stage.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
flowchart LR
  A[Question and scope] --> B[Access and infrastructure]
  B --> C[Project scaffold]
  C --> D[Data intake and documentation]
  D --> E[Exploration and notebooks]
  E --> F[Reusable scripts or pipeline]
  F --> G[Review and communication]
  G --> H[Archive and handoff]






The point of the workflow is not bureaucracy. The point is to make a project understandable and rerunnable at every stage.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
1. Question and scope
Start by writing down:

the scientific question,
the unit of analysis,
the key inputs,
the expected outputs,
and the likely compute or security constraints.

If this is not written down, folder structure and code quality will not save the project.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
2. Access and infrastructure
Decide where the work belongs before coding heavily:

public or low-risk exploratory work may fit on Cannon or another standard environment,
regulated or contract-restricted work may require FASSE or another secure environment,
and heavy preprocessing or simulation should be designed for scheduled compute, not a laptop session.

Create the project in the environment where it will actually live.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
3. Project scaffold
Set up the repository immediately. A good starting point is:
project-name/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── data/
├── data-raw/
├── code/
├── notebooks/
├── reports/
├── figures/
├── results/
└── renv.lock or environment.yml
The NSAPH handbook recommends a very similar repository layout because it separates code, figures, reports, results, and data from the start.1

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
4. Data intake and documentation
Before analysis scales up, document:

where the authoritative data lives,
who controls access,
whether you should symlink instead of copy,
the update cadence,
key identifiers,
and what cannot leave the secure environment.

If a dataset deserves an analysis, it deserves a data dictionary.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
5. Exploration and notebooks
Do early exploration in notebooks or scripts that explain:

what you are testing,
what assumptions you are making,
what looks wrong,
and what should happen next.

R for Data Science treats communication as part of the data science loop, not a final wrapper placed on top of “real” analysis. That is the right model for lab work too.2

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
6. Reusable scripts or pipeline
Once a workflow stabilizes, move repeated logic out of exploratory notebooks and into:

scripts,
functions,
parameterized reports,
or a pipeline tool such as targets, make, or snakemake.

The trigger is simple: if you expect to rerun it, scale it, or hand it off, it should not live only in an exploratory notebook cell.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
7. Review and communication
Every project should be able to produce:

a short README,
one current notebook or report that explains the analysis status,
a reproducible path from data inputs to results,
and a Git history that reflects real milestones.

This makes supervisor meetings, methods review, and cross-training materially easier.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
8. Archive and handoff
Before a paper, graduation, or staff transition:

confirm the repo can be cloned and run from scratch,
confirm restricted data are not in Git,
confirm documentation identifies canonical data locations,
render a final report or project website,
and record ownership for future maintenance.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
Minimal viable workflow
If you are overwhelmed, do these five things first:

Work on the correct cluster.
Put the analysis in Git.
Use relative paths.
Keep one notebook or report up to date.
Write enough documentation that another lab member could restart the project next week.

title: Golden Lab Data Workflows
text:
Lab defaults


1. Remote + reproducible beats local + manual
If work matters, it should run on supported infrastructure and be re-runnable from code.


2. Code is the source of truth
Results, figures, and tables are products of the workflow. The lasting record is the code and documentation that created them.


3. Analysis should be narrated
Use notebooks and reports to explain what you did, why you did it, and what should happen next.


4. Robust habits compound
Version control, clean project structure, and minimal documentation reduce technical debt for everyone.

title: Golden Lab Data Science Workflows
section: What students should be able to do
text:
What students should be able to do
By the end of onboarding, a student should be able to:

Start a project on the correct FASRC system and choose an appropriate storage location.
Create a reproducible project structure with notebooks, scripts, and dependency tracking.
Read shared datasets without making unnecessary copies.
Push analysis code and documentation to GitHub without exposing restricted data.
Ask for help with enough context that someone else can reproduce the problem.

title: Golden Lab Data Science Workflows
text:
Suggested reading path

Read Start Here for the workflow philosophy and first-week checklist.
Read Workflow Roadmap for the full research lifecycle.
Use the guide pages as reference while you are actively working on a project.
Keep Reference Library open when you need the official docs.

title: Contribution Guide
text: We welcome contributions to the Golden Lab Data Science Handbook! If you have suggestions for improvements, additional content, or corrections, please feel free to submit a pull request or open an issue on our GitHub repository.

title: Contribution Guide
text:
How to Contribute
This site is built using Quarto, which is a powerful publishing and technical writing tool that allows us to create a static website from markdown files that can easily integrate prose, code, and visualizations. If you want to contribute, you will primarily be doing so by editing the markdown files in the repository.

title: Contribution Guide
text:
Getting Started
There are two ways to contribute to this documentation:

Directly on GitHub: You can edit the markdown files directly on GitHub by navigating to the file you want to edit and clicking the pencil icon. This is a quick and easy way to make small changes or corrections.
Locally in Your Own Environment: For larger contributions, you may want to clone the repository to your local environment, make your changes, and then push them back to GitHub. This allows you to test your changes locally before submitting a pull request.


Editing on GitHubEditing Locally


Editing on GitHub is straightforward. Simply navigate to the file you want to edit, click the pencil icon, make your changes, and then commit them directly to the repository. This method is ideal for small edits, such as fixing typos or updating links. However, even for small contributions, it’s best to provide a clear paper trail for what you changed and why, and so we require that all contributions — even small ones — be made through GitHub Issues and Pull Requests1. If this is your first time contributing to an open-source project, this as great low-stakes opportunity to try out this workflow!

Navigate to GitHub. In your browser of choice, go to the GitHub repository: GoldenPlanetaryHealthLab.github.io
Find “Issues”: Click the Issues tab second from the left.
Open an Issue: Click the green “New Issue” button on the right.
Pick the Documentation Template: Click “🧪 New Data Science Tutorial or Documentation”
Fill Out the Template: A template will appear for you to fill out. Please provide a clear title and description of your contribution, however small, and any relevant links or references. For irrelevant fields, simply write “N/A”.
Assign Yourself: Assign yourself to the issue using the “Assignees” section on the right. This is what will enable us to award your contribution to you in the all-contributors table and give you credit for your work.
Edit the File: Navigate to the file you want to edit, click the pencil icon, and make your proposed changes using markdown, Quarto features, and/or code chunks as needed. You can preview your changes using the “Preview changes” tab to make sure everything looks good.
Commit Your Work: Commit your changes using the dialogue box to the repository. In the commit message, please use the GitHub Issues linking syntax to reference the issue number (e.g., “Fixes #123”) to link your changes to the issue you created. Importantly, make sure to select the option to commit your changes to a new branch and create a pull request. This will automatically open a pull request for your changes, which will be reviewed by the maintainers of the repository.




GitHub Issue Template




Working locally2 is more involved and requires familiarity with git and the command line. However, this is a useful skill to have and is the standard workflow for contributing to open-source scientific projects. Strap in, and let’s get you set up to contribute like a pro!

Create the Issue on GitHub to Work On: Before you start working on your contribution, it’s important to create an issue on GitHub to track your work. This allows the maintainers to review your proposed changes and provides a clear record of your contribution. Navigate to the GitHub repository, click on the “Issues” tab, and create a new issue using the “🧪 New Data Science Tutorial or Documentation” template.
Set up your local environment for git: On FASRC, git comes pre-installed, but in case you can’t find it, type which git. If you get a path back, you’re good to go. If not, load the git module with module load git.
Set Up Your Local Environment for GitHub: GitHub has a handy CLI tool called gh that makes it easy to interact with GitHub from the command line, including creating issues, making Pull Requests, and managing your authentication. To install gh on FASRC, see the instructions here #TODO, and return to this tutorial.

You might be aware that FASRC also has some guidance on working with GitHub here. Frankly, their instructions are a bit dated and their recommendations are less user friendly than just using the gh CLI tool, so we recommend following the instructions above instead of those on FASRC’s website.

Clone the Repository: Find an appropriate directory to work in (since this repository will likely have many individuals contributing to it and very little data or computation, it’s safe to do in your home directory) and run gh clone command to clone the repository to your local environment:

gh repo clone GoldenPlanetaryHealthLab/GoldenPlanetaryHealthLab.github.io
# activate the environment for the repository if applicable

# source .venv/bin/activate # if you have a virtual environment set up for this repository

Create a Local Issue Branch: It’s best practice to create a new branch for your changes, so using the gh CLI tool, create a new branch based on the issue you created in step 1:

gh issue develop [ISSUE NUMBER] --checkout

Make Your Changes: Now that you have your local environment set up and a new branch created, you can make your changes to the markdown files using your preferred text editor or IDE. Be sure to save your and commit your changes frequently, and test them locally if necessary.
Preview Your Changes: If you want to preview your changes locally before submitting a pull request, you can use the quarto preview command. This will start a local server where you can view your changes in a web browser.
Create a Pull Request: Once you’re happy with your changes, you can create a pull request using the gh CLI tool:

gh pr create



GitHub Pull Request In Action

title: Contribution Guide
section: Closing the Issue and Submitting a Pull Request
text:
Closing the Issue and Submitting a Pull Request
To complete the process, review the Pull Request, adding relevant information to the Pull Request description, and then submit it for review. The maintainers will review your changes and may provide feedback or request changes before merging your contribution into the main branch.

Quarto is a scientific and technical publishing system built on top of Pandoc, and driven by Markdown. Quarto Markdown files (.qmd) can contain prose, code, and visualizations, and are incrementally rendered into a static website by first evaluating the YAML front matter, running code chunks, and then rendering the markdown into HTML. All of this is wrapped up in the command quarto render.
To build the website locally, you must of course have Quarto installed on your machine/environment. Once you have Quarto and the repo, run the command quarto preview in the terminal while in the root directory of the repository, and Quarto will run quarto render and start a local server where you can view the website in your browser.
On GitHub, we have set up a GitHub Action that automatically runs quarto render and deploys the website’s HTML files to GitHub Pages whenever a commit is added to the main branch. This is why we enforce that all contributions go through GitHub Issues and Pull Requests, even small ones: it ensures that there is a clear record of what changed and why, and allows us to review changes before they are merged and deployed to the live website.
So the next time you want to add new information to the site, and feel like learning something about CI/CD, take a closer look at the GitHub Action that runs on this repository in .github/workflows/publish.yml, and the accompanying set up in the Repo Settings to learn how this process works! You might even want to set up a similar workflow for your own project or personal website.

title: Contribution Guide
text:
all-contributors Specification (Advanced Users Only)
We use the all-contributors specification to recognize and celebrate the contributions of everyone who has helped with this project. This includes not only code contributions but also documentation, design, and other forms of support e.g. feedback, issue submission and conversation, etc.
You can automate the process of adding contributors by using the all-contributors CLI tool. On Mac, install npm first:
brew install npm node
Then, use the following command to add a contributor:
npm install all-contributors-cli
The project is already initialized with all-contributors, so you can start adding contributors right away. You can add contributors from the command line. For example, to add a contributor with the username octocat who has contributed code and documentation, you would run:
npx all-contributors add octocat code,doc
And then create the contribution table with:
npx all-contributors generate
This will update the README.md file with the new contributor and their contributions. Commit and push this file to GitHub to see the changes reflected in the repository’s README.

title: Contribution Guide
text:
Footnotes


This is a common practice in open-source projects to ensure that all contributions are reviewed and documented properly. And, to your benefit, if you make changes using git your contributions will be automatically tracked and attributed to you, which is important for recognizing your work and giving you credit for your contributions.↩︎
While we use the word, “locally,” here, what we really mean is, “not on GitHub or on the cloud.” Locally can refer to your personal computer, or FASRC (which is what we expect you to use). The key point is that, “locally,” means, “you are directly interfacing with the machine where you will be making the edits.”↩︎

title: Start Here
text:
Most graduate students do not struggle because the science is impossible. They struggle because the workflow is fragile: files live on laptops, results are copied by hand, folders drift out of sync, and the explanation for a key decision is trapped in somebody’s memory.
The goal of this site is to make the robust path the normal path.

title: Start Here
text:
Most graduate students do not struggle because the science is impossible. They struggle because the workflow is fragile: files live on laptops, results are copied by hand, folders drift out of sync, and the explanation for a key decision is trapped in somebody’s memory.
The goal of this site is to make the robust path the normal path.

title: Start Here
text:
The working philosophy
The original presentation reduces to a few durable defaults:

prefer remote and reproducible workflows over local and manual ones,
keep code, not outputs, as the source of truth,
narrate analysis with notebooks and reports,
track change with Git and GitHub,
and invest early in structure so later work gets cheaper.

R for Data Science makes the same argument in a more general form: for larger projects and collaboration, the lasting record of an analysis should be the scripts, because scripts plus data can recreate the environment, while an IDE session alone cannot.1

title: Start Here
text:
Fragile versus robust workflows







Fragile workflow
Robust workflow




Lives on one machine
Lives on supported infrastructure and in version control


Depends on clicking and memory
Depends on scripts, notebooks, and documented steps


Stores only outputs
Stores the method that creates outputs


Breaks when paths, packages, or people change
Survives new machines, new users, and reruns

title: Start Here
section: What “source of truth” means in practice
text:
What “source of truth” means in practice
Treat these artifacts as the core record of a project:

A README that says what the project is and how to run it.
Scripts or notebooks that recreate the analysis.
A lockfile or environment spec such as renv.lock, environment.yml, or requirements.txt.
Data documentation that explains inputs, keys, units, and restrictions.
Git history that shows what changed and why.

Treat these as derived products:

figures,
rendered reports,
slides,
manuscripts,
and intermediate outputs that can be regenerated.

title: Start Here
text:
First-week checklist

Get cluster access and confirm you can log in through the correct FASRC pathway.
Create a repository for your project before the analysis becomes messy.
Create a minimal project scaffold with directories for code, data documentation, figures, reports, and results.
Build one small end-to-end example that reads data, runs one analysis step, and renders one output.
Write down where the authoritative data lives and what restrictions apply.
Push code and documentation to GitHub before asking for help.

title: Start Here
section: What the RSE is helping you do
text:
What the RSE is helping you do
The RSE role is not just “fix code when it breaks.” It is also to help students:

choose the correct compute environment,
avoid avoidable copies of large or regulated data,
standardize project layout,
move from ad hoc scripts to reusable workflows,
and lower the cost of collaboration across the lab.

title: Start Here
section: Common failure modes to avoid early
text:
Common failure modes to avoid early

Running long jobs on a laptop because it feels faster to start.
Hard-coding absolute paths that only work for one person.
Saving important objects only in the interactive environment.
Renaming scripts to final_v2_really_final.
Pushing data to GitHub because the .gitignore was never set up.
Waiting to document the project until after the analysis is already confusing.

text:

Go to Projects and Reproducibility if you are setting up a new analysis repo.

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
The presentation starts from a practical point: do the science on infrastructure designed for the science. That usually means remote compute, durable storage, and workflows that can survive browser crashes, laptop failures, or long runtimes.
FASRC’s current recommendation is consistent with that framing: for R users, the recommended starting point is RStudio Server on Open OnDemand.1

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
The presentation starts from a practical point: do the science on infrastructure designed for the science. That usually means remote compute, durable storage, and workflows that can survive browser crashes, laptop failures, or long runtimes.
FASRC’s current recommendation is consistent with that framing: for R users, the recommended starting point is RStudio Server on Open OnDemand.1

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Choose the right access path








Need
Best starting point
Why




Interactive R work
Open OnDemand RStudio Server
Fast setup, browser-based, simplest package installation


Interactive shell work
SSH or OOD shell access
Best for CLI tools, Git, file inspection, and job submission


Long or heavy compute
Slurm batch jobs
Reproducible, schedulable, and not tied to your browser session


Secure interactive work
FASSE OOD
Required when data restrictions demand the secure environment



According to the FASRC OOD documentation, the dashboard gives you file browsing, job status, job submission, remote desktops, and interactive apps such as RStudio, Matlab, and Jupyter.2

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Cannon versus FASSE
Use the environment that matches the data and policy requirements:

Cannon is the standard FASRC cluster for general research computing.
FASSE is the secure environment for work that requires stronger data protection.

The NSAPH handbook explicitly distinguishes Cannon from FASSE this way and uses FASSE for regulated data workflows.3

text:
Recommended pattern for students

Connect to the correct VPN realm.
Start an interactive session for exploration.
Prototype a small slice of the analysis.
Move stable steps into scripts.
Schedule heavier work through Slurm or another batch mechanism.
Keep code and documentation in GitHub; keep restricted data on approved storage.

Storage

Prefer canonical locations over duplicate copies
The NSAPH handbook recommends symlinking shared analytic datasets instead of making new copies.1 That is a strong general rule for lab work:

keep the canonical dataset in an approved shared location,
point to it from the project when possible,
and only create project-owned copies when the analysis truly requires them.

text:
Data categories to think about







Category
Typical handling




Public data
Can often be downloaded or scripted directly inside the project


Shared internal data
Reference canonical lab locations and document ownership


Restricted data
Keep in approved secure storage; never push to public Git hosting


Derived analytic data
Rebuild from code when possible; document version and inputs

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Minimum metadata for each dataset
For each important input, record:

dataset name,
canonical path or URL,
data owner or steward,
update cadence,
key identifiers,
units and missing-value conventions,
access restrictions,
and the script that creates downstream analytic tables.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Data dictionaries and process diagrams
The NSAPH workflow documentation explicitly recommends data diagrams and data dictionaries.2 Both are worth standardizing in the lab:

use a data dictionary when many variables need explanation,
use a flow diagram when multiple sources are joined or filtered,
and store both close to the repository rather than in private notes.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Raw data should stay raw
Do not edit raw data files in place. Instead:

leave the original input untouched,
write cleaning or harmonization code in code/,
save documented derived outputs to approved locations,
and make sure the transformation path is reproducible.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Pins, manifests, and lightweight registries
For small derived artifacts, shared models, parameter tables, or published outputs, use a lightweight registry pattern:

pins for R objects or shareable artifacts,
CSV or YAML manifests for file inventories,
and README-based tables for canonical paths and ownership.

This gives you traceability without treating every dataset like application code.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Git rules for data
Commit:

schemas,
dictionaries,
manifests,
scripts,
notebooks,
and small example data when policy allows it.

Do not commit:

restricted raw data,
large transient intermediates,
files in scratch,
or anything that violates the DUA or institutional policy.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
A .gitignore pattern worth keeping
data/
*.csv
*.parquet
*.fst
*.rds
Refine this by project, but start from the assumption that code and documentation belong in Git while sensitive or bulky data usually do not.

title: Golden Lab Data Science Workflows
text:
Lab Principles


1. Remote & reproducible beats local & manual 🌐
If your work matters, it should run on any infrastructure, and be re-runnable from code — not clicks.


2. Code is the source of truth 💎
Results, figures, and tables are merely products of the workflow. The lasting record — and scientific truth underlying them — is in the code and documentation that created them.


3. Analysis should be narrated 📇
Use notebooks and reports to explain what you did, why you did it, and what should happen next.


4. Robust habits compound 🌱
Version control, clean project structure, and clear documentation reduces technical debt for your colleagues — and your future self.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
flowchart TD
  A[Question and scope ⛰️] --> B[Preliminary infrastructure 🚧]
  B --> C[Individual Project scaffold 🏗️]
  C --> D[Data Lake Access 🌊]
  D --> E[Data Analysis and Notebooks 🔬]
  E --> F[Reusable Scripts and Pipelines 🗜️]
  F --> G[Collaborative Review and Communication 📊]
  F --> E
  G --> F
  G --> H[Publish and Archive 🗃️]







Let’s take some time to discuss and review an ideal workflow. This is a high-level overview of the lifecycle of a data science project in the lab — we’ll discuss actual implementation in the Guides sections. While the lifecycle may not be a strict linear process, it is a good starting point for understanding how to structure and prioritize your work at each stage, and what decisions you should be making to make your research reproducible.

flowchart TD

  F --> G[Collaborative Review and Communication 📊]

Let’s take some time to discuss and review an ideal workflow. This is a high-level overview of the lifecycle of a data science project in the lab — we’ll discuss actual implementation in the Guides sections. While the lifecycle may not be a strict linear process, it is a good starting point for understanding how to structure and prioritize your work at each stage, and what decisions you should be making to make your research reproducible.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
1. Question and Scope ⛰️
These are the foundational elements of your science that you may discuss with your PI, your colleagues, your collaborators, etc. You may start by simply writing down things like:

the scientific question,
the unit of analysis,
the key inputs,
the expected outputs,
and the likely compute or security constraints.

Much of this work can exist in Google Drive as meeting notes, lecture notes, and other communications. This is where you establish your motivation and high-level scientific field of interest.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
2. Access and infrastructure
Before writing any code, decide where the work belongs:

publicly available data and identifiable data at or below security level 2 is appropriate for the standard FASRC environment, sometimes called CANNON; the vast majority of our lab work will fit this description;
restricted data at security level 3 or above may require FASSE and a secure compute environment.

Additionally, think about which tools are best for the job. In this lab, we use R and Python, but the workflow principles apply to either and any other language you may use.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
3. Project scaffold
#todo create template repo and link here Set up the repository immediately. A good starting point is:
project-name/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── data/
├── data-raw/
├── code/
├── notebooks/
├── reports/
├── figures/
├── results/
└── renv.lock or environment.yml
The NSAPH handbook recommends a very similar repository layout because it separates code, figures, reports, results, and data from the start.1

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
4. Data intake and documentation
Most of our lab data exists on FASRC, and is specially curated to be accessible and secure. Recall the Golden Lab project folder structure on Google Drive:

You’ll notice a locked folder called 4. Datasets. This is the raw data that Chris has been collecting over the years, and it is the starting point for all of our analyses. In order to interact with this data on FASRC, we set up a mirroring system that periodically syncs the raw data from Google Drive to FASRC using the rclone utility. We call this the data_lake, and it underlies how you can access the data on FASRC without having to download it from Google Drive, so you can be assured that it is up to date and secure2.

For any local individual project, the first step will be to symlink the project area’s raw data to your project repository. Fortunately, most data sources will come with an accompanying data distribution package that provides a function to do this for you. If it does not yet exist, let Tinashe know.






NoteWhy Do We Symlink Datasets?



“Symlinking” means to create a symbolic link to the original data file, rather than copying it. This has several advantages:

It saves disk space, since you are not creating duplicate copies of the data.
It ensures that you are always working with the most up-to-date version of the data, since the symlink “points” to the original file that is being synced from Google Drive.
It maintains the security and integrity of the data, since you are not creating additional copies that could be accidentally modified or shared.
It encourages you to create intermediate outputs and final results in your project repository without affecting the original raw data.

By symlinking raw datasets, you can safely interact with raw data without being concerned about any data management tasks, allowing you to focus on the analysis and scientific questions at hand.





Nepal Climate Health Data Lake





Data Lake Symlinked to Local Individual Project

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
5. Exploration and notebooks 🔬
Once you’ve symlinked the raw data, you can start doing the fun science. Notebooks are a great tool for this stage, since they allow you to write code, run it, and see the results all in one place. They also allow you to write markdown text to explain your thinking and document your process. I’ve heard it said that, “writing is thinking,”3 and writing code should be no different. As you write code, you should simultaneously be writing down:

what you are testing,
what assumptions you are making,
what looks right, and what looks wrong,
and what should happen next.

For example, R for Data Science treats communication as part of the data science loop, not a final wrapper placed on top of “real” analysis. That is the right model for lab work too.4
We recommend using Quarto for your notebooks, since it allows you to write in R, Python, or both in the same document, and it has great support for rendering and sharing your work. You can also use Jupyter Notebooks or R Markdown Notebooks if you prefer. The important thing is to choose a tool that allows you to document your thinking and share your work with others.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
6. Reusable scripts or pipeline
Once a workflow stabilizes, move repeated logic out of exploratory notebooks and into:

scripts,
functions,
parameterized reports,
or a pipeline tool such as targets, make, or snakemake.

The trigger is simple: if you expect to rerun it, scale it, or hand it off, it should not live only in an exploratory notebook cell.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
7. Review and communication 📊
As you go through the exploratory data analysis phase, you’ll likely be meeting with your PI and collaborators to discuss analysis results and brainstorm ideas. It wouldn’t be very wise to show up to a meeting with half a dozen arbitrarily named Python scripts or R scripts, and expect everyone in the meeting to understand your work. Instead, you should collect and export digestible findings that can be presented in meetings5.
If you’ve structured your project correctly, each time you meet with your supervisor or collaborators, your project should have:

an up to date README at the base of the repository that explains the project, how to run the code, and when it was last updated,
one or more current notebooks or reports that explain the current analysis status,
the reproducible path from data inputs to results in a pipeline, and
a git history that reflects real milestones.

This makes supervisor meetings, methods review, and cross-training materially easier.






NoteThe Data Science Loop



You may notice that the workflow has cyclical edges between Data Analysis & Notebooks, Reusable Scripts & Pipelines, and Review & Communication. This Data Science Loop is a continuous cycle of analysis, interpretation, and communication. As you analyze your data, you’ll likely uncover new questions and insights that will lead to new analyses. This is a normal part of the scientific process, and it’s important to embrace it. By structuring your project in a way that allows for easy communication and collaboration, you can make the most of this iterative process and produce high-quality science.



R4DS Data Science Loop

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
8. Archive and handoff
Before a paper, graduation, or staff transition:

confirm the repo can be cloned and run from scratch,
confirm restricted data are not in Git,
confirm documentation identifies canonical data locations,
render a final report or project website,
and record ownership for future maintenance.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
Minimal viable workflow
If you are overwhelmed, do these five things first:

Work on the correct cluster.
Put the analysis in Git.
Use relative paths.
Keep one notebook or report up to date.
Write enough documentation that another lab member could restart the project next week.

title: Start Here
text:
Most graduate students do not struggle to manage their research because the science is impossible. They struggle because their workflows are fragile: important files live on personal laptops, results of analyses are copied by hand, versions of scripts are scattered, and the explanation for a key decision is trapped in the memory of someone who has long left the lab.
The goal of this site is to make the robust path the easy path.

Fragile versus robust workflows







Fragile workflow
Robust workflow

title: Start Here
section: What “source of truth” means in practice
text:
What “source of truth” means in practice
If I handed you a scientific paper — any paper — and asked you where the real core of the scientific finding was, where would you point me to? The conclusion? No, the conclusion is an interpretation. The figures? Again, no — they are a product of a particular analysis. The reasoning in the introduction? Not really — it is just a motivating narrative. The real core of the scientific finding is that the authors told you HOW to get from the data to the conclusion. This is found in the methods section, and more specifically the exact procedure that creates the result, repeatably, from the data. It is the repeatability of the method producing the same result that makes it science, in the first place after all. If I cannot repeat your experiment, then I cannot verify your claim, and your conclusion is therefore not scientific.






ImportantIn the modern data science era, the methods section is the code, and code is the source of truth. The code is the core record of an experiment, which puts it at the heart of what you should be writing, documenting, and sharing as a scientist. It is — without question — your most valuable asset.






Ask yourself: if someone stole your laptop today and tossed it into the Charles River, Google went under and your Scholar ID disappeared, and Dr. Strange erased everyone’s memory of you, would your contribution to science survive? If the answer is “no,” then you have a fragile workflow. If the answer is “yes,” then you have a robust workflow.
To ensure you would survive this totally plausible catastrophe, treat these basic artifacts as invaluable:

A README that clearly describes what the project is and how to run it.
Scripts and/or notebooks that describe how the analysis is conducted.
A lockfile or environment specification such as rv.lock, environment.yml, or requirements.txt that tell us which tools and versions of those tools are needed.
Data provenance that explains where the data came from.
git history that clearly shows what changed and why, each step of the way.

On the other hand, derived products such as:

figures,
rendered reports,
slides,
manuscripts,
and intermediate outputs

Should all be treated as ephemeral details that can be regenerated whenever asked You should never lose sleep over these things because your code should be able to regenerate them at a moment’s notice.

text:

title: Start Here
text:
7 Start-Here Checklist
So now that we know our end goal and working philosophy, what prerequisites do we need to be able to achieve them? Here is a checklist of the most important things to have done:


Complete the Harvard CITI training and share your completion certificate with Chris. This is required for you to have access to the data we work with, and to be able to contribute to the research. There are two modules:


Social and Behavioral/Biomedical Human Subject Training
Internet Privacy and Security (IPS) Training

If you’ve never registered for CITI training before, you will need to create an account and affiliate with Harvard University. When you are asked to select a course, select “Social and Behavioral/Biomedical Human Subject Training,” and then select the modules listed above. If you have already completed CITI training for another institution, you can log in with your existing account, affiliate with Harvard University, and then add the two modules listed above to your existing training. If your training is still valid, send Chris a copy of your completion certificate. If you’ve never taken them or your training has expired, you will need to retake the modules and share the new certificate with Chris.


Officially be inducted into the Golden Lab by filling out the new member form — we use this to grant you access to:


Google Drive, for managing shared documents and non-code, office-oriented work 📁
GitHub, for managing and collaborating on code 🔌
Slack, for work-related communication about projects, sharing information, and questions 💬
WhatsApp, for non-work-related communication and socializing 🎭



Set up your FASRC account by following the instructions available here:


Carefully read the instructions; you’ll use the Account Request Tool to request an account using your HarvardKey, and when you are asked to specify the PI, select “Christopher Golden,” and “Nutrition,” as the department. Chris will receive a notification and approve your request as soon as possible, but don’t hesitate to send him an email letting him know you have submitted the request and are excited to get started.




Follow the workflow roadmap guide on the next page to set up your first project and learn how to do reproducible data science.

title: Start Here
section: Why Can’t I Just Do This On My Laptop By Myself? Why Are You Making Me Do this?
text:
Why Can’t I Just Do This On My Laptop By Myself? Why Are You Making Me Do this?
As the lab’s Research Software Engineer, my role is not just “fix code when it breaks.” It is also to help students:

adhere to security, privacy, and ethical standards for working with human subjects data at Harvard,
set up the optimal compute environment to speed up their work,
avoid making critical mistakes that duplicate, mismanage, expose, or worse, lose sensitive data,
eliminate future technical debt by adhering to best practices for standardizing project layout,
lower the cost of collaboration across and outside of the lab,
focus on the science instead of wrestle with technical issues

title: Start Here
section: 9 Example of Common Failures You’ll Avoid by Following This Guide
text:
9 Example of Common Failures You’ll Avoid by Following This Guide

Waiting all day for a piece of code to run on your laptop that could have run in 10 minutes on the cluster,
Losing your work due to inadequate backup strategies,
Writing hardcoded scripts that only work for one person,
Saving important objects only in the interactive environment, only to lose them when the session ends,
Infinitely renaming scripts final_v2_really_final every time you receive feedback from your collaborators,
Accidentally pushing sensitive data to GitHub because the .gitignore was never set up,
Waiting to document the project until after you’ve already forgotten what you did and why,
Struggling to get help because you don’t have a reproducible example of the problem,
Using the wrong version of a package and getting different results than your collaborators…

And much, much more.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
3. Individual Project Scaffold 🏗️

An individual project has three components: the Google Drive Data Lake, the local project repository, and GitHub repository.
On Google Drive, we maintain a strict folder structure for each Golden Lab research project.



GDrive Projects





GDrive Project Folder Structure


You’ll use these GDrive projects as information hubs for your individual research projects, which will be implemented on FASRC. For example, while the Golden Lab is broadly studying “Climate-Smart Public Health in Madagascar,” your individual project may be particularly interested in the relationship between heat stress and hospitalizations in Northeast Madagascar.
It’s important to maintain this structure so that there is a clear separation of concerns between high-level project management, the day-to-day work of data analysis, and the tools that each of those tasks require. Google Drive is a great place for taking notes with Docs, storing PDF files, and collaborating on office files manuscripts and presentations. FASRC is a great place for writing code, running analyses, and storing data and outputs. You wouldn’t want to write and store code files in Google Drive, and you wouldn’t want to maintain Google Docs files notes on FASRC — hence the clear separation of these environments1.
Even if you don’t have the dataset or specific hypotheses yet, now is the time to set up the local project repository. A good starting point might look like this:
project-name/
├── README.md       <- a clear project description and instructions for how to run the code
├── .gitignore      <- to exclude data, results, and other large files from `git`
├── data-outputs/   <- for intermediate and final data outputs  
├── data-raw/       <- for linking your project to the data lake
├── code/           <- for plain code scripts, functions, and pipeline code
├── notebooks/      <- for exploratory notebooks and reports
├── manuscript/     <- for manuscript drafts, figures, and tables
└── lockfile        <- for package management and reproducibility
In addition, you should set up a GitHub repository for your project, and link it to your local repository.



GitHub Repository


For each language, this may look slightly different, but the principle is the same. The sooner you set up the project scaffold, the more likely you are to to adhere to its conventions. If you wait until after you have a dataset, have written a few scripts, etc., you may find yourself copying and pasting code and data, missing git commits, or setting up inconsistent file paths, which is harder to maintain and reuse in future. Setting this up might feel like a chore, but we’ve gone ahead and done the work for you. Using our GitHub templates, you can easily create a new repository with the correct structure and fill in the blanks.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
2. Preliminary Infrastructure 🚧
Before writing any code, decide where the work belongs:

publicly available data and identifiable data at or below security level 2 is appropriate for the standard FASRC environment, sometimes called CANNON; the vast majority of our lab work will fit this description;
restricted data at security level 3 or above may require FASSE and a secure compute environment.

Additionally, think about which tools are best for the job. In this lab, we use R and Python, but the workflow principles apply to either and any other language you may use.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
4. Data Lake Access 🌊
Most of our lab data exists on FASRC, and is specially curated to be accessible and secure. Recall the Golden Lab project folder structure on Google Drive:

You’ll notice a locked folder called 4. Datasets. This is the raw data that Chris has been collecting over the years, and it is the starting point for all of our analyses. In order to interact with this data on FASRC, we set up a mirroring system that periodically syncs the raw data from Google Drive to FASRC using the rclone utility. We call this the data_lake, and it underlies how you can access the data on FASRC without having to download it from Google Drive, so you can be assured that it is up to date and secure2.

For any local individual project, the first step will be to symlink the project area’s raw data to your project repository. Fortunately, most data sources will come with an accompanying data distribution package that provides a function to do this for you. If it does not yet exist, let Tinashe know.






NoteWhy Do We Symlink Datasets?



“Symlinking” means to create a symbolic link to the original data file, rather than copying it. This has several advantages:

It saves disk space, since you are not creating duplicate copies of the data.
It ensures that you are always working with the most up-to-date version of the data, since the symlink “points” to the original file that is being synced from Google Drive.
It maintains the security and integrity of the data, since you are not creating additional copies that could be accidentally modified or shared.
It encourages you to create intermediate outputs and final results in your project repository without affecting the data lake.

By symlinking raw datasets, you can safely interact with raw data without being concerned about any data management tasks, allowing you to focus on the analysis and scientific questions at hand.





Nepal Climate Health Folder of the Data Lake on GDrive





Nepal Climate Health Folder of the Data Lake in a Local Individual Project on FASRC

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
6. Reusable scripts and Pipelines 🗜️
Most scientific papers aren’t a once-off analysis. As you dig into the data, you’ll raise new questions that’ll require you to wrangle it in numerous ways — mutating variables, joining tables, and creating intermediate objects and visualizations that are used in different ways. You may also need to rerun analyses with different parameters, or on different subsets of the data. If you are doing this work in a single exploratory notebook or script, it can quickly become messy and hard to maintain.
We recommend starting off your project with a pipeline tool that allows you to record the progression of your data manipulations. Ideally, at the end of the project, a single pipeline should be able to take the raw data as input and produce all of the final results, including tables, figures, and manuscript drafts.
Coding pipelines are sometimes called DAGs, or Directed Acyclic Graphs, because they are a series of steps that are connected in a directed way (i.e., one decision leads to the next). This is a great way to structure your code, since it allows you to see the flow of your analysis and how each investigation (notebook) uncovered new challenges or ideas that led to the final result. It also allows you to easily rerun parts of the analysis if you need to make changes or update the data.
Once a workflow stabilizes, move repeated logic out of exploratory notebooks and into:

scripts,
functions,
parameterized reports,
all managed by a pipeline orchestration tool such as targets, pytask, or snakemake.

The trigger is simple: if you expect to rerun it, scale it, or hand it off, it should not live only in an exploratory notebook cell.






NoteIs A Pipeline Orchestration Tool Overkill?



We get it, you’re not trying to be a data engineer — you just want to publish papers. But the reality is that if you are doing data science, you are doing data engineering. If you are writing code that manipulates data, even in just a handful of lines, you are engineering a solution to a problem. The question is not whether you should use a pipeline orchestration tool, but rather when you should start using one.
The sooner you do this, the better. If your project were to scale (which it almost certainly will), you’ll have a hard time retroactively moving code around into a pipeline, and you’ll likely end up with a messy codebase that may not be reproducible. Adding a pipeline orchestrator early on costs very little, and it allows you to focus on the science without worrying about the engineering later.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
8. Publish and Archive 🗃️
When you and your collaborators are ready to publish or transition ensure that your project is in a state that can be easily shared and maintained. This includes:

confirming the repository can be cloned from GitHub and run from scratch,
confirming restricted data are not in Git,
confirming documentation identifies canonical data locations,
rendering a final report or project website,
and recording ownership for future maintenance.

Depending on your publication venue, you may also want to archive your code and data in a more permanent repository, such as Dataverse or Zenodo, and link it to your publication. Additionally, you may want to add credential badges to your GitHub repository to communicate the quality of your work, such as FAIR software, a Zenodo DOI badge, or a GitHub Actions CI/CD badge that shows the status of your pipeline.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
5. Data Analysis and Notebooks 🔬
Once you’ve symlinked the raw data, you can start doing the fun science. Notebooks are a great tool for this stage, since they allow you to write code, run it, and see the results all in one place. They also allow you to write markdown text to explain your thinking and document your process. I’ve heard it said that, “writing is thinking,”3 and writing code should be no different. As you write code, you should simultaneously be writing down:

what you are testing,
what assumptions you are making,
what looks right, and what looks wrong,
and what should happen next.

For example, R for Data Science treats communication as part of the data science loop, not a final wrapper placed on top of “real” analysis. That is the right model for lab work too.4
We recommend using Quarto for your notebooks, since it allows you to write in R, Python, or both in the same document, and it has great support for rendering and sharing your work. You can also use Jupyter Notebooks or R Markdown Notebooks if you prefer. The important thing is to choose a tool that allows you to document your thinking and share your work with others.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
Conclusion
While this workflow may seem daunting at first, it gets easier with practice. The key is to start early and be consistent. By following this roadmap, you can ensure that your work is reproducible, maintainable, and scalable.

title: Start Here
text:
Principle 1: Remote and Reproducible Workflows 🌐

What is the difference between a fragile workflow and a robust workflow?








Fragile workflow
Robust workflow




Lives on one machine
Lives on supported infrastructure and in version control


Depends on clicking and memory
Depends on scripts, notebooks, and documented steps


Stores only outputs
Stores the method that creates outputs


Breaks when paths, packages, or people change
Survives new machines, new users, and reruns

title: Start Here
text:
Principle 2: The Source of Truth

What does, “source of truth,” mean in practice?

If I handed you a scientific paper — any paper — and asked you where the real core of the scientific finding was, where would you point me to? The conclusion? No, the conclusion is an interpretation. The figures? Again, no — they are a product of a particular analysis. The reasoning in the introduction? Not really — that’s just a motivating narrative. The real core of the scientific finding is that the authors told you HOW to get from the data to the conclusion. This is found in the methods section, and more specifically the exact procedure that creates the result, repeatably, from the data. It is the repeatability of the method producing the same result that makes it science, in the first place after all. If I cannot repeat your experiment, then I cannot verify your claim, and your conclusion is therefore not scientific.






Important



In the modern data science era, the methods section is the code, and code is the source of truth. The code is the core record of an experiment, which puts it at the heart of what you should be writing, documenting, and sharing as a scientist. It is — without question — your most valuable asset.


Ask yourself: if someone stole your laptop today and tossed it into the Charles River, Google went under and your Scholar ID disappeared, and Dr. Strange erased everyone’s memory of you, would your contribution to science survive? If the answer is “no,” then you have a fragile workflow. If the answer is “yes,” then you have a robust workflow.
To ensure you would survive this totally plausible catastrophe, treat these basic artifacts as invaluable:

A README that clearly describes what the project is and how to run it.
Scripts and/or notebooks that describe how the analysis is conducted.
A lockfile or environment specification such as rv.lock, environment.yml, or requirements.txt that tell us which tools and versions of those tools are needed.
Data provenance that explains where the data came from.
git history that clearly shows what changed and why, each step of the way.

On the other hand, derived products such as:

figures,
rendered reports,
slides,
manuscripts,
and intermediate outputs

Should all be treated as ephemeral details that can be regenerated whenever asked You should never lose sleep over these things because your code should be able to regenerate them at a moment’s notice.

title: Start Here
text:
Principle 3: Narrating Your Analysis
David McCullough is credited with the quote, “Writing is thinking. To write well is to think clearly. That’s why it’s so hard.” If you’ve ever found yourself struggling to articulate an idea on paper that just moments before felt crystal clear in your head, you know exactly what he means. Donald Knuth, the father of algorithm analysis and creator of TeX, felt similarly about programming, and has evangelized literate programming — the practice of writing code that is meant to be read by humans, and that tells a story about the analysis — for decades.3. Today, we’re fortunate to have tools like R Markdown, Jupyter Notebooks, and Quarto that make it easy to write code that is meant to be read by humans, and that tells a story about the analysis. If there is anywhere appropriate for literate programming to improve our work, it’s in science, where the goal is not just to get the right answer, but to explain how we got there and why it matters. By narrating our analysis with notebooks, we afford our brains the opportunity to think through the analysis step by step, making our work more transparent, reproducible, and less prone to error or bias.

title: Start Here
text:
Principle 4: Robust Habits Compound 🌱

Why invest in engineering as a scientist?

Technical debt is a term that software engineers use to describe the cost of maintaining and updating code that was written in a way that is not scalable or maintainable. Because scientists are often less motivated to invest in robust software design patterns, they can end up with a lot of technical debt in their code, which can make it difficult to understand, reproduce, or extend as the science progresses. This can lead to wasted time and effort, or worse, lost insight. By investing early in robust habits such as version control, clean project structure, and clear documentation, you can reduce technical debt and make your work easier to manage as your project grows. These habits compound over time, making it easier to maintain and extend your work in the future.

text:
Principle 3: Analysis Should Be Narrated 📇

Why should scientists narrate their thinking?

David McCullough is credited with the quote, “Writing is thinking. To write well is to think clearly. That’s why it’s so hard.” If you’ve ever found yourself struggling to articulate an idea on paper that just moments before felt crystal clear in your head, you know exactly what he means. title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
7. Collaborative Review and Communication 📊
As you go through the exploratory data analysis phase, you’ll likely be meeting with your PI and collaborators to discuss analysis results and brainstorm ideas. It wouldn’t be very wise to show up to a meeting with half a dozen arbitrarily named Python scripts or R scripts, and expect everyone in the meeting to understand your work. Instead, you should collect and export digestible findings that can be presented in meetings5.
If you’ve structured your project correctly, each time you meet with your supervisor or collaborators, your project should have:

an up to date README at the base of the repository that explains the project, how to run the code, and when it was last updated,
one or more current notebooks or reports that explain the current analysis status,
the reproducible path from data inputs to results in a pipeline, and
a git history that reflects real milestones.

This makes supervisor meetings, methods review, and cross-training materially easier.






NoteThe Data Science Loop



You may notice that the workflow has cyclical edges between Data Analysis & Notebooks, Reusable Scripts & Pipelines, and Review & Communication. This Data Science Loop is a continuous cycle of analysis, interpretation, and communication. As you analyze your data, you’ll likely uncover new questions and insights that will lead to new analyses. This is a normal part of the scientific process, and it’s important to embrace it. By structuring your project in a way that allows for easy communication and collaboration, you can make the most of this iterative process and produce high-quality science.



R4DS Data Science Loop

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Version control is not only for backup. It is how the lab understands change, reviews methods, and avoids conflicting local copies of the same project.
The presentation frames Git and GitHub as the tool for traceability, and that is exactly right: commit history should explain what changed, when, and why.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Daily Git habits
Use Git for active work, not just for milestone dumps.

Create the repository early.
Commit small, coherent changes.
Use commit messages that describe the decision or milestone.
Push regularly so the work is visible and recoverable.
Keep data out of Git unless policy clearly allows it.

The NSAPH handbook also recommends pushing code regularly, especially before asking for help or presenting work.1

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Branches and pull requests
For solo projects, a lightweight branch workflow is still useful:

Make a branch for risky or substantial work.
Open a pull request when the method or output changes meaningfully.
Use review comments to discuss code, assumptions, and documentation gaps.
Merge only when the branch leaves the repository in a reproducible state.

title: Collaboration and Support
section: What a good commit message looks like
text:
What a good commit message looks like
Better:

add first pass of temperature exposure merge
document MCBS variable cleaning assumptions
move repeated model code into helper function

Worse:

updates
stuff
final final version

title: Collaboration and Support
section: How to ask for help effectively
text:
How to ask for help effectively
R for Data Science recommends preparing a minimal reproducible example when you are stuck.2 That is one of the highest-leverage habits students can learn.
When asking the RSE or another lab member for help, include:

the repo or branch,
the exact script or notebook,
the command you ran,
the full error message,
the smallest reproducible example you can produce,
and any data access constraints that prevent direct debugging.

title: Collaboration and Support
section: What to send before a meeting
text:
What to send before a meeting
For technical meetings, send:

the current notebook or report,
a short status note,
open questions,
and links to the relevant code or pull request.

This shifts meetings away from “what happened?” and toward “what decision do we need to make?”

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Code review checklist
Review for:

reproducibility,
path portability,
data leakage risk,
hidden assumptions,
duplicated code,
and missing documentation.

Do not review only for style.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Support ladder
Use this order when possible:

Check the project README and this site.
Search the official docs for the tool you are using.
Reduce the problem to a minimal example.
Open an issue or send a focused help request.
Escalate to synchronous help when the blocker persists.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
The real goal
The goal is not to make every student a Git expert. The goal is to make the lab’s work inspectable, shareable, and recoverable.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
section: Scripts are the source of truth
text:
Scripts are the source of truth
The most important R4DS workflow recommendation is simple: for larger projects and collaboration, the source of truth should be the scripts, because scripts plus data recreate the environment.2
That generalizes beyond R:

in R, keep the workflow in scripts, Quarto notebooks, and package lockfiles,
in Python, keep it in scripts, notebooks, pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, or environment.yml,
and in either language, avoid workflows that depend on an IDE’s memory of prior objects.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Relative paths only
R4DS is explicit here: once you are inside a project, use relative paths, not absolute paths, because absolute paths hinder sharing.3
Good:
readr::read_csv(here::here("data", "analytic", "cohort.csv"))
Bad:
readr::read_csv("/Users/alice/Desktop/final_data/cohort.csv")

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
A practical project layout
project-name/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── data/
├── data-raw/
├── code/
├── notebooks/
├── reports/
├── figures/
├── results/
├── scratch/
└── renv.lock
Suggested meanings:

data/: pointers, symlinks, small metadata, and lightweight project-owned data
data-raw/: import receipts, extraction scripts, or manifests for original inputs
code/: reusable scripts and functions
notebooks/: exploratory and explanatory Quarto notebooks
reports/: rendered outputs for meetings, manuscripts, or handoff
results/: small derived artifacts worth keeping
scratch/: local pointers to transient compute outputs when appropriate

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Notebook versus script versus function
Use a notebook when you need to explain reasoning.
Use a script when you need to run a stable step repeatedly.
Use a function when logic is reused in more than one place.
Use a pipeline when many steps depend on each other and reruns are expensive.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Environment management
The presentation already points to renv and conda, and that is still the right baseline.
For R:
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
For Python, commit an environment.yml or pyproject.toml and keep it current.
The goal is not perfection. The goal is that another person, or future you, can reconstruct the environment without guesswork.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Configuration, not hard-coded infrastructure
If a project depends on shared paths outside the repository, put those paths in configuration.
default:
  analytic_root: "/n/shared/project-data"
  scratch_root: "/n/netscratch/project-name"
Then use that configuration in code instead of repeating infrastructure paths throughout the analysis.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
section: Save outputs from code, not with the mouse
text:
Save outputs from code, not with the mouse
If a figure matters, it should be saved from the script or notebook that created it. R4DS makes this point for figures, but the same logic applies to tables, model objects, and reports: reproducible export beats manual copy-paste every time.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
When to move beyond notebooks
Move to a pipeline or more modular structure when:

the same notebook is rerun many times with small edits,
multiple students touch the same workflow,
the compute cost makes reruns expensive,
or you can no longer tell which cells are still authoritative.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
A strong minimum standard
Every active project should have:

a readable README,
a committed .gitignore,
a current notebook or report,
relative paths,
and an environment specification.

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Harvard University’s computing cluster is called FASRC (Faculty of Arts and Sciences Research Computing), and is one of the largest, most powerful compute clusters on the East Coast. It provides scalable computing resources, secure environments for sensitive data, and a shared infrastructure that supports collaboration and reproducibility. As discussed in Principle 1, we treat HPC as the default environment for data science in the lab. If your project works on HPC, it is more likely to be reproducible, shareable, and scalable. If it only works on your local machine, which only has settings and configurations specific to you, it is more likely to break when you or someone else tries to run it again in the future. By working this way, we intentionally introduce a small amount of friction upfront, to save a lot of time and headache in the future.
But don’t let that intimidate you! The FASRC team has done a great job of making the cluster accessible and user-friendly, and there are many ways to interact with it that require close to zero setup or expertise. The goal of this guide is to help you find the right access path for your needs, and to give you some practical tips and examples for working on HPC.

text:
4 Choose the right access path


Tab 1Tab 2


content


content











Need
Best starting point
Why




Interactive R work
Open OnDemand RStudio Server
Fast setup, browser-based, simplest package installation


Interactive shell work
SSH or OOD shell access

Long or heavy compute
Slurm batch jobs

According to the FASRC OOD documentation, the dashboard gives you file browsing, job status, job submission, remote desktops, and interactive apps such as RStudio, Matlab, and Jupyter.1

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
5 Cannon versus FASSE
Use the environment that matches the data and policy requirements:

Cannon is the standard FASRC cluster for general research computing.
FASSE is the secure environment for work that requires stronger data protection.

The NSAPH handbook explicitly distinguishes Cannon from FASSE this way and uses FASSE for regulated data workflows.3

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
5 Recommended pattern for students

Connect to the correct VPN realm.
Start an interactive session for exploration.
Prototype a small slice of the analysis.
Move stable steps into scripts.
Schedule heavier work through Slurm or another batch mechanism.
Keep code and documentation in GitHub; keep restricted data on approved storage.

title: HPC and Infrastructure
section: 6 Storage tiers and what they are for
text:
6 Storage tiers and what they are for








Storage
Use it for
Do not use it for




Home directory
Small personal config and lightweight project files
Large shared analysis outputs


Shared lab/project storage
Canonical project code and approved shared data access
Personal scratch or duplicate copies


/n/netscratch
High-I/O temporary intermediates
Permanent storage or anything that is not reproducible


GitHub
Code, docs, templates, issues, and small text-based metadata
Raw data, large binaries, or restricted files



FASRC documents /n/netscratch as temporary, high-performance, not backed up, and subject to a 90-day retention policy.2

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
7 FASRC tips that save time

Use Open OnDemand when you want the simplest path into interactive compute.
Use the “My Interactive Sessions” area to reconnect to running browser-based sessions.
Use Chrome or Firefox for OOD; FASRC warns that Safari may not behave well for some apps.
If package installation fails in FASSE RStudio Server, check whether you need to set the FASRC proxy variables first.
Remember that RStudio Server on OOD is convenient, but FASRC notes that module load and sbatch are not available inside that app.
