title: Data Access and Management
section: Prefer canonical locations over duplicate copies
text:
Prefer canonical locations over duplicate copies
The NSAPH handbook recommends symlinking shared analytic datasets instead of making new copies.1 That is a strong general rule for lab work:

keep the canonical dataset in an approved shared location,
point to it from the project when possible,
and only create project-owned copies when the analysis truly requires them.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
A simple data lifecycle





flowchart LR
  A[Raw source data] --> B[Document source and restrictions]
  B --> C[Create project pointers or symlinks]
  C --> D[Build analytic data reproducibly]
  D --> E[Generate figures, tables, and models]
  E --> F[Archive documentation and outputs]

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Data categories to think about







Category
Typical handling




Public data
Can often be downloaded or scripted directly inside the project


Shared internal data
Reference canonical lab locations and document ownership


Restricted data
Keep in approved secure storage; never push to public Git hosting


Derived analytic data
Rebuild from code when possible; document version and inputs

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Minimum metadata for each dataset
For each important input, record:

dataset name,
canonical path or URL,
data owner or steward,
update cadence,
key identifiers,
units and missing-value conventions,
access restrictions,
and the script that creates downstream analytic tables.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Data dictionaries and process diagrams
The NSAPH workflow documentation explicitly recommends data diagrams and data dictionaries.2 Both are worth standardizing in the lab:

use a data dictionary when many variables need explanation,
use a flow diagram when multiple sources are joined or filtered,
and store both close to the repository rather than in private notes.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Raw data should stay raw
Do not edit raw data files in place. Instead:

leave the original input untouched,
write cleaning or harmonization code in code/,
save documented derived outputs to approved locations,
and make sure the transformation path is reproducible.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Pins, manifests, and lightweight registries
For small derived artifacts, shared models, parameter tables, or published outputs, use a lightweight registry pattern:

pins for R objects or shareable artifacts,
CSV or YAML manifests for file inventories,
and README-based tables for canonical paths and ownership.

This gives you traceability without treating every dataset like application code.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Git rules for data
Commit:

schemas,
dictionaries,
manifests,
scripts,
notebooks,
and small example data when policy allows it.

Do not commit:

restricted raw data,
large transient intermediates,
files in scratch,
or anything that violates the DUA or institutional policy.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
A .gitignore pattern worth keeping
data/
*.csv
*.parquet
*.fst
*.rds
Refine this by project, but start from the assumption that code and documentation belong in Git while sensitive or bulky data usually do not.

text:
Data access requests should answer these questions

What dataset do you need?
Where is the canonical source?
What approvals or training are required?
What environment are you allowed to use?
Can the data be shared, or only the code?
Who should maintain the access instructions when the project ends?

What is the difference between a fragile workflow and a robust workflow?








Fragile workflow
Robust workflow




Lives on one machine

title: Start Here
section: 4 Principle 2: Code is The Source of Truth 💎
text:
4 Principle 2: Code is The Source of Truth 💎

What does, “source of truth,” mean in practice?

If I handed you a scientific paper — any paper — and asked you where the real core of the scientific finding was, where would you point me to? The conclusion? No, the conclusion is an interpretation. The figures? Again, no — they are a product of a particular analysis. The reasoning in the introduction? Not really — that’s just a motivating narrative. The real core of the scientific finding is that the authors told you HOW to get from the data to the conclusion. This is found in the methods section, and more specifically the exact procedure that creates the result, repeatably, from the data. It is the repeatability of the method producing the same result that makes it science, in the first place after all. If I cannot repeat your experiment, then I cannot verify your claim, and your conclusion is therefore not scientific.






Important



In the modern data science era, the methods section is the code, and code is the source of truth. The code is the core record of an experiment, which puts it at the heart of what you should be writing, documenting, and sharing as a scientist. It is — without question — your most valuable asset.


Ask yourself: if someone stole your laptop today and tossed it into the Charles River, Google went under and your Scholar ID disappeared, and Dr. Strange erased everyone’s memory of you, would your contribution to science survive? If the answer is “no,” then you have a fragile workflow. If the answer is “yes,” then you have a robust workflow.
To ensure you would survive this totally plausible catastrophe, treat these basic artifacts as invaluable:

A README that clearly describes what the project is and how to run it.
Scripts and/or notebooks that describe how the analysis is conducted.
A lockfile or environment specification such as rv.lock, environment.yml, or requirements.txt that tell us which tools and versions of those tools are needed.
Data provenance that explains where the data came from.
git history that clearly shows what changed and why, each step of the way.

On the other hand, derived products such as:

figures,
rendered reports,
slides,
manuscripts,
and intermediate outputs

Should all be treated as ephemeral details that can be regenerated whenever asked You should never lose sleep over these things because your code should be able to regenerate them at a moment’s notice.

title: Start Here
text:
5 Principle 3: Analysis Should Be Narrated 📇

Why should scientists narrate their thinking?

David McCullough is credited with the quote, “Writing is thinking. To write well is to think clearly. That’s why it’s so hard.” If you’ve ever found yourself struggling to articulate an idea on paper that just moments before felt crystal clear in your head, you know exactly what he means. Donald Knuth, the father of algorithm analysis and creator of TeX, felt similarly about programming, and has evangelized literate programming — the practice of writing code that is meant to be read by humans, and that tells a story about the analysis — for decades.3. Today, we’re fortunate to have tools like R Markdown, Jupyter Notebooks, and Quarto that make it easy to write code that is meant to be read by humans, and that tells a story about the analysis. If there is anywhere appropriate for literate programming to improve our work, it’s in science, where the goal is not just to get the right answer, but to explain how we got there and why it matters. By narrating our analysis with notebooks, we afford our brains the opportunity to think through the analysis step by step, making our work more transparent, reproducible, and less prone to error or bias.

title: Start Here
text:
6 Principle 4: Robust Habits Compound 🌱

Why invest in engineering as a scientist?

technical debt is a term that software engineers use to describe the cost of maintaining and updating code that was written in a way that is not scalable or maintainable. Because scientists are often less motivated to invest in robust software design patterns, they can end up with a lot of technical debt in their code, which can make it difficult to understand, reproduce, or extend as the science progresses. This can lead to wasted time and effort, or worse, lost insight. By investing early in robust habits such as version control, clean project structure, and clear documentation, you can reduce technical debt and make your work easier to manage as your project grows. These habits compound over time, making it easier to maintain and extend your work in the future.

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
2 Some Lingo
Let’s clear the air of some jargon that you might encounter when working with HPC:







Term
Definition




HPC
High Performance Computing. A general term for powerful computing resources that can handle large-scale data analysis and modeling.


FASRC
Faculty of Arts and Sciences Research Computing. The computing cluster used at Harvard.


Cannon
The standard FASRC cluster for general research computing. Synonymous with FASRC for all intents and purposes.


FASSE
The secure environment for work that requires stronger data protection (L3 and above).


Open OnDemand (OOD)
A web-based interface that provides access to interactive apps, file browsing, and job submission tools on FASRC.


Shell/SSH (noun)
A command-line interface used to interact with the operating system and run commands, including managing files and submitting jobs. Common shells include bash (default for FASRC) and zsh (default for macOS).


SSH (verb)
To connect to a remote machine using the SSH protocol (e.g., “SSH into the cluster”).


Slurm
A workload manager that allows you to schedule and manage batch jobs on the cluster.


Batch Job
A task given to the system in the form of a script. You submit it to the queue and it runs when resources are available. For example, a Python script that you want to run on the cluster without manual intervention.



Another confusing collection of jargon is the interchangeable use of environments, software, and modules. Let’s clarify these:

Software: The actual programs and tools that you use to do your work, such as R, Python, or specific packages. For example, R version 4.2.0 is a piece of software.
Modules: A system for managing software on HPC clusters. Modules allow you to load and unload specific versions of software and their dependencies. For example, you might run module load R/4.2.0 to load R version 4.2.0 and its associated libraries into your environment.
Computing Environment: The overall setup of your computing context, including the hardware, operating system, software packages, and modules that are available to you. For example, the Cannon cluster is an environment, and the FASSE cluster is a different environment with different security settings and software availability.
Environment variables: These are key-value pairs that are set in your shell and can be accessed by your programs. They often contain configuration settings, such as paths to software or proxy settings. Importantly, environment variables are “set” by the shell when it starts, are specific to the user who starts the shell, and are inherited by any processes that are started from that shell. They are not global settings for the cluster, but rather specific to your session. For example, when I log into the cluster, my shell automatically sets environment variables that point to the R and Python installations, and I can also set my own environment variables for things like proxy settings or custom paths. These settings are made in the file ~/.bashrc, which you can modify to create a customized environment. Take a moment to look at your own environment variables by running env in your shell, to see what is set by default, and cat ~/.bashrc to see your customizations.







Tip



For your purposes, “Software” refers broadly to the programs and tools you use, “Modules” are the FASRC-managed software packages you load, “Computing Environment” is the overall context of your work on HPC, and “project environment,” is the specific configuration of software and settings that your local project relies on to run successfully.

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
3 Interface
We recommend two main interfaces for working on FASRC: Open OnDemand (OOD) and SSH. OOD is a web-based interface that provides access to interactive apps like RStudio Server, Jupyter, and Matlab, as well as file browsing and job submission tools. SSH is a command-line interface that allows you to connect to the cluster from your terminal and use the command line to run commands, manage files, and submit jobs.

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Harvard University’s computing cluster is called FASRC (Faculty of Arts and Sciences Research Computing), and is one of the largest, most powerful compute clusters on the East Coast. It provides scalable computing resources, secure environments for sensitive data, and a shared infrastructure that supports collaboration and reproducibility. As discussed in Principle 1, we treat HPC as the default environment for data science in the lab. If your project works on HPC, it is more likely to be reproducible, shareable, and scalable. If it only works on your local machine, which only has settings and configurations specific to you, it is more likely to break when you or someone else tries to run it again in the future. By working this way, we intentionally introduce a small amount of friction upfront, to save a lot of time and headache in the future.
But don’t let that intimidate you! The FASRC team has done a great job of making the cluster accessible and user-friendly, and there are many ways to interact with it that require close to zero setup or expertise. The goal of this guide is to help you find the right access path for your needs, and to give you some practical tips and examples for working on HPC.

title: Contribution Guide
section: How the Website is Actually Built
text:
How the Website is Actually Built
Quarto is a scientific and technical publishing system built on top of Pandoc, and driven by Markdown. Quarto Markdown files (.qmd) can contain prose, code, and visualizations, and are incrementally rendered into a static website by first evaluating the YAML front matter, running code chunks, and then rendering the markdown into HTML. All of this is wrapped up in the command quarto render.
To build the website locally, you must of course have Quarto installed on your machine/environment. Once you have Quarto and the repo, run the command quarto preview in the terminal while in the root directory of the repository, and Quarto will run quarto render and start a local server where you can view the website in your browser.
On GitHub, we have set up a GitHub Action that automatically runs quarto render and deploys the website’s HTML files to GitHub Pages whenever a commit is added to the main branch. This is why we enforce that all contributions go through GitHub Issues and Pull Requests, even small ones: it ensures that there is a clear record of what changed and why, and allows us to review changes before they are merged and deployed to the live website.
So the next time you want to add new information to the site, and feel like learning something about CI/CD, take a closer look at the GitHub Action that runs on this repository in .github/workflows/publish.yml, and the accompanying set up in the Repo Settings to learn how this process works! You might even want to set up a similar workflow for your own project or personal website.
